back_translation: Saul was a year old when he became king, and he reigned over Israel two years.

Saul chose for himself three thousand men from Israel — two thousand were with Saul at Michmash and in the hill country of Bethel, and one thousand were with Jonathan at Gibeah of Benjamin. And the rest of the people he sent away, each one to his own tent.
And Jonathan struck the Philistine garrison that was at Geba, and the Philistines heard. And Saul blew the ram's horn throughout the land, saying, 'Let the Hebrews hear.'
And all Israel heard the word, 'Saul has struck the Philistine garrison, and Israel has also become a stench to the Philistines.' And the people were summoned to follow Saul at Gilgal.

And the Philistines assembled to fight against Israel — thirty thousand chariots, six thousand horsemen, and foot soldiers as numerous as the sand on the seashore. And they went up and encamped at Michmash, east of Beth-aven.

And the men of Israel saw that they were in distress, for the people were being hard-pressed, and the people hid themselves in caves, in shelters, in rock-piles, in underground vaults, and in cisterns.
And some Hebrews crossed the Jordan into the land of Gad and Gilead. But Saul remained at Gilgal, and all the people were quaking behind him.
And he waited seven days until the time appointed by Samuel, but Samuel did not come to Gilgal, and the people began to scatter from him.
And Saul said, 'Bring me the burnt offering and the peace offerings.' And he offered up the burnt offering.
And just as he had finished offering the burnt offering — behold, Samuel arrived, and Saul went out to greet him in order to bless him.
And Samuel said, 'What have you done?' And Saul said, 'When I saw that the people had scattered from me, and you had not come at the appointed time, and the Philistines were gathering at Michmash —'
'I thought, Now the Philistines will descend upon me at Gilgal, and I have not entreated the face of the LORD. So I forced myself, and I offered up the burnt offering.'

And Samuel said to Saul, 'You have acted foolishly. You have not kept the commandment of the LORD your God which He commanded you. Otherwise, the LORD would have established your kingdom over Israel forever.

But now your kingdom will not endure. The LORD has sought a man according to His own heart, and He has commanded him to be ruler over His people, because you have not kept what the LORD commanded you.'
And Samuel arose and went up from Gilgal to Gibeah of Benjamin, and Saul numbered the people who remained with him — about six hundred men.
And Saul and Jonathan his son, and the people who were with them, were staying at Geba of Benjamin, while the Philistines encamped at Michmash.
And the raiders went out from the camp of the Philistines in three divisions — one division turned toward Ophrah, to the land of Shual,
and another division turned toward Beth-horon, and another division turned toward the border road that overlooks the Valley of Zeboim, toward the wilderness.
And no blacksmith could be found in all the land of Israel, for the Philistines had said, 'Lest the Hebrews make swords or spears.'
And all Israel had to go down to the Philistines to sharpen — each man — his plowshare and his mattock and his axe and his sickle.
And the charge was a pim for the plowshares and for the mattocks, and a third of a shekel for the three-pronged forks and for the axes, and for repointing the oxgoad.
And on the day of battle, no sword or spear was found in the hand of all the people who were with Saul and with Jonathan — but they were found for Saul and for Jonathan his son.
And the garrison of the Philistines went out toward the pass of Michmash.
